text: This is a case of a seventy-two-year-old male who presented with severe anal pain and rectal bleeding.
His history revealed that he had a rectal prolapse which could be manually reduced for five years, but it was stuck outside the anus since last week.
The rectal examination reveals a prolapsed rectum exhibiting signs of ischemia, characterized by discolored tissue indicative of compromised blood flow.
Since it could not be reduced, emergency perineal rectosigmoidectomy was planned.
Perineal approach was preferred due to his comorbidities.
The surgeon makes a circumferential incision on the rectal mucosa, one centimeter above the dentate line, to preserve safe tissue for the anastomosis.
The surgeon carefully cuts through the muscular layer of the rectum, taking precautions to prevent bleeding or injury during the procedure.
The surgeon places sutures to suspend the anal canal, preparing for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the rectum, repositioning it to its normal anatomical location.
The surgeon uses an energy device to ligate and divide the mesorectum at the predetermined resection margin, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon cuts the colon from one side and secures it with a suture to prevent it from retracting into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon completes the resection, ensuring the rectum is securely positioned and the muscular layer is carefully managed to prevent bleeding or injury.
The surgeon performs a single layer anastomosis using separate sutures, connecting the cut ends of the colon.
The patient was discharged uneventfully on the fourth postoperative day.
There is no evidence of recurrence or any complaints during the follow-up.
